# Core-Unit Documentation

> This file provides authoritative context about the Core-Unit of the No-Code System (NCS) for any LLM
> working within this codebase. Read it fully before generating code, making architectural
> decisions, or answering questions about the system. Terms defined here take precedence over
> general assumptions.

---
## Purpose

The NCS Core is the central engine of an LLM-powered Low-Code/No-Code platform. It enables
end-users without programming knowledge to create dynamic web artifacts — primarily websites
and landing pages — through natural-language interaction. The LLM interprets the user's intent,
generates the artifact's source code, provisions the required infrastructure (including the CMS),
and orchestrates the full deployment pipeline. The user never writes or sees code directly.

---

## Glossary

### Artifact
- An Artifact is a digital application produced by the NCS.
- Every Artifact has a **type** (e.g. `website`, `landing-page`).
- An Artifact consists of:
  - **Source code** — the generated files that implement the application (e.g. a web
    framework project).
  - **Metadata** — name, type, creation date, current lifecycle state.
  - **Deployment configuration** — one or more configuration files specific to the active
    Deployment Target (e.g. a Compose file, a Kubernetes manifest, a cloud run spec).
- An Artifact is always associated with exactly one Deployment Target.
- An Artifact can be in one of the following lifecycle states:
  - `draft` — being set up or generated, not yet runnable
  - `sandbox` — actively running in Sandbox Mode for development and iteration
  - `stopped` — was running but is currently stopped
  - `archived` — no longer actively used

### Website (Artifact Type)
- A Website is an Artifact of type `website`.
- It is built with a dynamic web framework (e.g. Next.js, Nuxt, SvelteKit).
- It consists of multiple pages, a navigation structure, and dynamic content sections.
- Content is managed independently of the page layout via the CMS (Directus).

### Landing Page (Artifact Type)
- A Landing Page is an Artifact of type `landing-page`.
- It is a single-page artifact optimized for a specific conversion goal (e.g. product
  presentation, sign-up).
- It may be built with the same web framework as a Website or as a standalone static/SSR page.
- Content is managed via the CMS (Directus), same as a Website.

### Deployment Target
- The Deployment Target is the infrastructure platform on which Artifacts are compiled, run,
  and served.
- The NCS controls the Deployment Target programmatically — the user does not interact with
  it directly.
- **The Deployment Target is selectable.** The NCS is designed to support multiple Deployment
  Target backends (e.g. local container runtime, remote VM, cloud platform). The active target
  is determined by the NCS configuration, not hard-coded into Artifact source code.
- The deployment configuration files and operational commands the NCS generates are specific
  to the active Deployment Target. All target-specific details are encapsulated in the
  `.adu/deployment-targets/` templates (see "Starter Template and Repository Layout").
- Artifact source code itself must remain target-agnostic; only the deployment configuration
  layer is target-specific.

### No-Code System (NCS)
- The NCS is the overall system through which the user interacts via natural language to
  create and manage Artifacts.
- Its responsibilities are:
  1. **Intent capture** — understand what the user wants to build.
  2. **Code generation** — produce the source code for the Artifact via LLM.
  3. **CMS provisioning** — set up and configure Directus for the Artifact (collections,
     fields, initial content).
  4. **Deployment orchestration** — start, stop, rebuild, and update Artifacts on the active
     Deployment Target.
  5. **Iteration support** — allow the user to refine the Artifact through further
     natural-language instructions, resulting in code modifications and live updates (in
     Sandbox Mode).
- The NCS is targeted at end-users: people with no knowledge of web development, deployment
  infrastructure, CMS administration, or programming. All complexity must be hidden behind
  the NCS interface.

### Sandbox Mode
- Sandbox Mode is the **development runtime** of an Artifact — the mode in which it is
  actively built and iterated upon.
- In Sandbox Mode:
  - The Artifact is **running live** on the active Deployment Target so the user can preview
    it in real time.
  - The Artifact's **source code lives on disk** (on the host) and is made available to the
    running process, so file changes on the host are immediately visible inside the runtime.
  - When the LLM modifies source files, the running framework's **hot-module replacement
    (HMR)** or **dev-server watch mode** picks up the changes automatically — no full restart
    is needed for code-only changes.
  - If **dependencies change** (e.g. a new package is added to `package.json`), the runtime
    environment must be **rebuilt** before the change takes effect.
  - Sandbox Mode corresponds to the framework's own `development` mode (e.g. `next dev`,
    `nuxt dev`, `vite dev`).
  - The **Directus CMS service is always running** alongside the app process in Sandbox Mode,
    providing the content API that the frontend consumes.
- Sandbox Mode is distinct from a future "production" deployment, which would involve a build
  step and a production-optimized environment.

---

## Deployment Target Specifics

The active Deployment Target is defined by the `.adu/deployment-targets/` templates. The NCS reads
these templates to understand how to start, stop, rebuild, and update Artifacts — it never
assumes a specific technology.

### Service Layout
A standard Artifact stack consists of three logical services, regardless of the target:
- **App service** — runs the web framework's dev server in Sandbox Mode.
- **CMS service** — runs Directus, providing the headless content API and admin UI.
- **Database service** — provides persistence for the CMS.

The deployment configuration files in `.adu/deployment-targets/` define concretely how these services
are wired together on the active target.

### Source Code Mounting (Sandbox Mode)
- The Artifact's source code directory on the host is made available to the app service's
  runtime so that file writes are reflected immediately without a full restart.
- Package/dependency directories (e.g. `node_modules`) live inside the runtime environment,
  not on the host, to avoid cross-platform issues.
- Directus database files and uploaded assets are stored in persistent volumes so that CMS
  data survives restarts.

### Dependency Changes
- When the LLM adds or removes dependencies (modifies `package.json` or equivalent), the
  app service environment must be **rebuilt**.
- The NCS is responsible for detecting dependency changes and triggering a rebuild
  automatically.
- Code-only changes (`.js`, `.ts`, `.vue`, `.svelte`, `.css`, etc.) never require a rebuild.
- Directus schema and content changes are applied via the Directus API at runtime and never
  require a service restart.

### Port Exposure
- The app service exposes the framework's dev-server port (e.g. 3000, 5173) so the user can
  open a preview in a browser.
- The Directus CMS service exposes port **8055** for both internal service-to-service
  communication and external admin UI access.
- The NCS manages port allocation to avoid conflicts between multiple simultaneously running
  Artifacts.

### Environment Variables and Secrets
- All secrets (API tokens, admin credentials, database passwords) are stored in a `.env` file
  on the host and injected into services at startup by the deployment configuration.
- Secrets must never appear as plain-text literals in source code or deployment configuration
  files — only as references to environment variable names (e.g. `${DIRECTUS_TOKEN}`).
- The `.env` file is never committed to version control.

---

## CMS: Directus

The CMS used in this system is **Directus** (https://directus.io). Directus is a headless CMS
that exposes all content via a REST and GraphQL API, which the generated frontend consumes.
The NCS provisions and configures Directus automatically — the user never interacts with
Directus's admin UI directly unless they choose to.

### Networking
This section describes the Core-System's services and their network-availability.

#### Internal network
| Service | Base URL                           | Notes                                                            |
|---------|------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------|
| CMS     | `http://pages-adu-directus-1:8055` | The CMS is available from this address from the internal network |

#### External network
| Service | Base URL                 | Notes                                                            |
|---------|--------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------|
| CMS     | `http://localhost/cms` | The CMS is available from this address from the external network |



### Authentication
- Directus is bootstrapped with an **admin email and password** generated by the NCS at
  provisioning time and stored as environment variables (injected via the deployment
  configuration).
- API access from the app service uses a **static API token** generated during provisioning,
  read exclusively from an environment variable (e.g. `DIRECTUS_TOKEN`). It must never be
  hard-coded in source files.

### SDK Usage
- All programmatic access to Directus from the generated frontend must use the **official
  Directus SDK** (`@directus/sdk`).
- The SDK must be initialized once (e.g. in a shared client module) and reused across the
  application.
- Example initialization pattern (TypeScript):
  ```ts
  import { createDirectus, rest, staticToken } from '@directus/sdk';

  const directus = createDirectus(process.env.DIRECTUS_URL!)
    .with(staticToken(process.env.DIRECTUS_TOKEN!))
    .with(rest());

  export default directus;
  ```
- The LLM must never construct raw `fetch` calls to the Directus REST API when the SDK
  covers the same operation.

### Data Modelling (Collections and Fields)
- Every content type required by an Artifact (e.g. blog posts, team members, hero sections)
  is represented as a **Directus collection**.
- Collections and fields are provisioned by the NCS via the Directus API immediately after
  the CMS service starts for the first time.
- The NCS generates the collection schema based on the LLM's interpretation of the user's
  intent and writes a provisioning script executed during Artifact setup.
- After initial provisioning, the LLM can **add, modify, or remove fields** in response to
  user instructions by calling the Directus Fields API — no service restart is required.

### Persistence
- Directus stores its data in the database service (PostgreSQL by default; SQLite is
  acceptable for lightweight Artifacts).
- All database files are stored in a persistent volume so that CMS content survives
  restarts and rebuilds.
- Uploaded media files (images, documents) are stored in a separate persistent volume.

---

## Starter Template and Repository Layout

Every new Artifact begins from a **Starter Template** — a pre-existing repository that the
coding agent uses as the initial working directory for the Artifact. The agent must never
start from an empty directory; the Starter Template is always the baseline.

### What the Starter Template Provides
- A fully configured web framework project (e.g. a Next.js or Nuxt project) with the
  directory structure, tooling, and base configuration already in place.
- Placeholder files and conventions that the agent fills in or extends. Starter Template
  files must not be overwritten or deleted without a deliberate, documented reason.
- A `package.json` with the core dependencies already declared. Additional dependencies
  required for a specific Artifact are added on top of this baseline.

### The `.adu` Folder
The `.adu` folder (located at the root of the repository) is the **NCS system folder**.
It contains templates, configuration, and scripts managed by the NCS — not by the generated
Artifact code itself. The agent must read and apply the contents of this folder; it must
never delete or structurally alter it.

Current contents of `.adu` (may grow over time — always inspect the actual folder on disk
before acting):

```
.adu/
├── deployment-targets/       # Deployment templates for the active Deployment Target
│   └── ...                   # Target-specific configuration files (e.g. Compose, manifests)
└── ...                       # Further templates added as the system evolves
```

#### `.adu/deployment-targets/` — Deployment Templates
- The files in `.adu/deployment-targets/` are the **canonical templates** for the Artifact's
  deployment configuration. When the NCS sets up a new Artifact, it copies and adapts these
  templates rather than generating deployment configuration from scratch.
- The agent must **always start from the `.adu/deployment-targets/` templates** when creating or
  updating deployment configuration for an Artifact. Deviations from the template are only
  permitted when a specific Artifact requirement makes it necessary and must be documented
  in a comment inside the file.
- When new deployment requirements arise (e.g. an additional sidecar service), the template
  itself should be updated so that future Artifacts benefit from the change.
- **The templates define the active Deployment Target.** The agent must not assume a specific
  target technology — it reads the templates to understand what is being used.

### Agent Workflow for a New Artifact
1. Read the `.adu` folder in full to understand the available templates and the active
   Deployment Target.
2. Copy the relevant files from `.adu/deployment-targets/` into the Artifact's root directory.
3. Fill in Artifact-specific values (service names, port numbers, environment variable
   references, volume names) based on the Artifact's metadata.
4. Extend or modify the Starter Template source files to implement the user's intent.
5. Never generate deployment configuration from scratch if templates exist in `.adu/`.

---

## LLM Responsibilities within the NCS Core

When operating as the engine of the NCS, the LLM has the following distinct roles:

1. **Intent Interpreter** — Translate the user's natural-language description into a
   structured understanding of what the Artifact should look like and do (pages, sections,
   content types, design intent).

2. **Template Reader** — Before writing any file, read the `.adu` folder in full to
   understand all available templates and the active Deployment Target. Always derive
   deployment configuration from `.adu/deployment-targets/` templates rather than generating
   it from scratch.

3. **Code Generator** — Produce or modify the source files of the Artifact (framework files,
   components, styles, content), always building on top of the Starter Template. Modifications
   must be **surgical** (change only what is necessary) to avoid breaking the running dev server.

4. **CMS Configurator** — Design the Directus collection schema for the Artifact's content,
   generate the provisioning script, and issue API calls to apply schema changes during
   iteration. The LLM must keep the frontend data model and the CMS schema in sync at all
   times.

5. **Deployment Configurator** — Copy and adapt deployment configuration from `.adu/deployment-targets/`
   templates for the full Artifact stack (app + CMS + database). Know when a full environment
   rebuild is required vs. when a file-write or API call alone suffices.

6. **Iteration Agent** — In Sandbox Mode, receive follow-up instructions from the user, map
   them to specific file changes or CMS API calls, apply those changes, and confirm that the
   dev server or CMS will reflect the update. Never regenerate the entire project from scratch
   for incremental changes.

---

## Key Constraints and Rules

- **The user never writes code.** Any response directed at the user must be in plain
  language, not code or configuration syntax.
- **Code goes to disk, not to chat.** Generated source files are written directly to the
  Artifact's directory on disk. Code is never pasted into the conversation.
- **Minimal diffs.** When iterating on an existing Artifact, only the affected files are
  changed. Rewriting the entire project for a minor change is forbidden.
- **Rebuild only when necessary.** An environment rebuild is only triggered when dependencies
  change (e.g. `package.json` modified), never for source code edits alone.
- **CMS changes via API.** Directus schema and content changes during iteration are always
  applied via the Directus API — never by editing raw database files or restarting services
  unnecessarily.
- **Single source of truth.** The deployment configuration files, the `.env` file, and the
  source code on disk are the authoritative state of an Artifact. The LLM must always reason
  from the current on-disk state, not from memory of a previous generation step.
- **No vendor lock-in.** Generated Artifact source code must use standard, framework-native
  conventions and must not depend on NCS-specific runtime libraries or abstractions.
- **Environment variables only.** All secrets (API tokens, admin credentials, database
  passwords) are stored exclusively in the `.env` file and injected by the deployment
  configuration. They must never appear as plain-text literals in source code or
  configuration files.
- **Always start from the Starter Template.** New Artifacts are never created from an empty
  directory. The Starter Template's structure and conventions are the mandatory baseline.
- **Always use `.adu/` templates.** Deployment configuration is always derived from
  `.adu/deployment-targets/` templates, never generated from scratch. The `.adu/` folder itself
  must never be deleted or modified by Artifact code generation.
- **Read `.adu/` before acting.** At the start of every new Artifact setup, the agent must
  inspect the full `.adu/` folder to discover all available templates before writing any file.
- **Deployment Target agnosticism.** Artifact source code must never reference or depend on
  a specific Deployment Target technology. Only the `.adu/deployment-targets/` layer is target-aware.
